"It is forbidden to benefit from this world without a blessing." Eating without a bracha treats G-d's world as ownerless â€” the bracha is our acknowledgment that everything belongs to G-d.

The six main blessings before food:
â€¢ Hamotzi (×”Ö·×žÖ¼×•Ö¹×¦Ö´×™×) â€” for bread made from any of the five grains (wheat, barley, spelt, oat, rye)
â€¢ Mezonot (×žÖ°×–×•Ö¹× ×•Ö¹×ª) â€” for other grain-based foods: cake, cookies, crackers, pasta, cereal
â€¢ HaGafen (×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ) â€” for wine or grape juice; the blessing is Borei pri ha'gafen (×‘Ö¼×•Ö¹×¨Öµ× ×¤Ö°Ö¼×¨Ö´×™ ×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ)
â€¢ HaEtz (×”Ö¸×¢Öµ×¥) â€” for fruit from a tree (apples, oranges, grapes, etc.)
â€¢ HaAdamah (×”Ö¸×Ö²×“Ö¸×žÖ¸×”) â€” for produce from the ground (vegetables, bananas, strawberries)
â€¢ Shehakol (×©Ö¶××”Ö·×›Ö¹Ö¼×œ) â€” for everything else: meat, fish, eggs, milk, cheese, water, candy, etc.

items.title: Brachot after eating or drinking
items.explanation:
After eating or drinking, we thank G-d with an after-blessing (bracha acharona). Which blessing you say depends on what you ate or drank â€” and whether you ate or drank enough, within the required time.

The after-blessings:

Birkat Hamazon (×‘Ö´Ö¼×¨Ö°×›Ö·Ö¼×ª ×”Ö·×žÖ¸Ö¼×–×•Ö¹×Ÿ â€” Grace After Meals):
â€¢ After eating bread (Hamotzi)
â€¢ A full multi-paragraph prayer of thanks; a Torah-level commandment (Deuteronomy 8:10)
â€¢ When three or more adult Jewish men eat bread together, Zimmun (×–Ö´×žÖ¼×•Ö¼×Ÿ â€” a formal invitation) is recited together before Birkat Hamazon
â€¢ A bentcher (×‘Ö¶Ö¼× Ö°×˜Ö°×©Ö¶××¨) is a small booklet containing the text â€” commonly found at Shabbat tables
â€¢ After a bread meal: if you ate at least a kezayit of bread within k'dei achilat pras, Birkat Hamazon (bentching) covers everything else you ate at that same meal â€” wine, meat, fruit, dessert, etc. You do not say separate after-blessings for those other foods.

Al HaMichya (×¢Ö·×œ ×”Ö·×žÖ´Ö¼×—Ö°×™Ö¸×” â€” Bracha Achat Me'ein Shalosh):
â€¢ After Mezonot â€” grain foods that are not bread: cake, crackers, pasta, cereal, etc. (wheat, barley, spelt, oat, rye)
â€¢ A condensed â€œone blessing like threeâ€ derived from Birkat Hamazon
â€¢ At the same sitting, if you also had enough wine/grape juice or enough shivat ha-minim tree fruits (below), use one Al HaMichya text with the inserted phrases (v'al hagafen v'al pri hagafen, v'al ha'etz v'al pri ha'etz) in your siddur

Al HaGafen (×¢Ö·×œ ×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ ×•Ö°×¢Ö·×œ ×¤Ö°Ö¼×¨Ö´×™ ×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ):
â€¢ After wine or grape juice â€” the full after-blessing is ×¢×œ ×”×’×¤×Ÿ ×•×¢×œ ×¤×¨×™ ×”×’×¤×Ÿ (the vine and the fruit of the vine), not ×¢×œ ×”×’×¤×Ÿ alone
â€¢ At least a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it (not Borei Nefashot)
â€¢ Same category as the before-blessing Borei pri ha'gafen (×‘Ö¼×•Ö¹×¨Öµ× ×¤Ö°Ö¼×¨Ö´×™ ×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ)
â€¢ Wine or grape juice only â€” fresh grapes eaten as fruit take Al HaEtz (see below), not Al HaGafen

Al HaEtz (×¢Ö·×œ ×”Ö¸×¢Öµ×¥ ×•Ö°×¢Ö·×œ ×¤Ö°Ö¼×¨Ö´×™ ×”Ö¸×¢Öµ×¥ â€” Bracha Achat Me'ein Shalosh):
â€¢ After shivat ha-minim (Seven Species) tree fruits: dates, figs, pomegranates, olives â€” and fresh grapes eaten as fruit (not grape juice)
â€¢ Fresh grapes: Because grapes are one of the Seven Species, eating a kezayit of fresh grapes always requires Al HaEtz (Me'ein Shalosh), not Borei Nefashot â€” whereas regular tree fruits like apples or oranges take Borei Nefashot only
â€¢ The full phrase is ×¢×œ ×”×¢×¥ ×•×¢×œ ×¤×¨×™ ×”×¢×¥ (the tree and the fruit of the tree) throughout the blessing â€” opening, thanks, and closing â€” not ×¢×œ ×”×¢×¥ alone
â€¢ Not for apples, oranges, bananas, berries, or other nonâ€“shivat ha-minim tree fruits â€” those use Borei Nefashot only
â€¢ A kezayit within k'dei achilat pras

Borei Nefashot (×‘Ö¼×•Ö¹×¨Öµ× × Ö°×¤Ö¸×©××•Ö¹×ª â€” "Who creates souls"):
â€¢ After most other foods: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, vegetables, and fruits that are not shivat ha-minim
â€¢ After most drinks other than wine/grape juice, if you drank a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
â€¢ The shortest after-blessing; can be memorized in minutes

How much food â€” k'dei achilat pras (eating):
â€¢ Generally need at least a kezayit (×›Ö°Ö¼×–Ö·×™Ö´×ª â€” about olive-sized; many use roughly 1 oz / 30 g)
â€¢ Eaten within k'dei achilat pras (×›Ö°Ö¼×“Öµ×™ ×Ö²×›Ö´×™×œÖ·×ª ×¤Ö°Ö¼×¨Ö·×¡ â€” the time to eat about half a loaf): lechatchila about 4 minutes; many poskim allow up to about 6â€“9 minutes bedi'eved (Shulchan Aruch / Mishnah Berurah)
â€¢ Less than a kezayit, or spread over longer than k'dei achilat pras: no after-blessing on that food

How much drink â€” k'dei shtiyat revi'it (drinking) â€” not the same as k'dei achilat pras:
Halacha often requires an action within a specific continuous timeframe. For eating a kezayit, that is k'dei achilat pras (minutes â€” see above). For drinking, it is much faster.

K'dei shtiyat revi'it (×›Ö°Ö¼×“Öµ×™ ×©Ö°××ªÖ´×™Ö·Ö¼×ª ×¨Ö°×‘Ö´×™×¢Ö´×™×ª) literally means the time it takes to drink a revi'it (about 3â€“5 fl oz / 86â€“150 ml depending on custom). It is a very short span â€” a few continuous seconds â€” used as a legal measurement for drinking.

For bracha acharona on a beverage:
â€¢ You need a revi'it drunk within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
â€¢ If you sip coffee slowly over 20 minutes, you did not drink the required volume within the required time â€” no after-blessing on that drink
â€¢ How many seconds? Poskim differ:
  â€” Sephardic / Rambam / Rav Ovadia Yosef: one continuous drink without removing the cup from your lips â€” the strictest view; halacha defines this qualitatively (not as a fixed second count), but it is only a brief uninterrupted span â€” often estimated at just a few seconds for a full revi'it
  â€” Ashkenaz / Mishnah Berurah: often estimated as a continuous sequence of normal gulps within 5â€“9 seconds max. To guarantee your obligation for an after-blessing, drink the beverage in a single, uninterrupted sequence.
â€¢ Cold drinks (water, juice): to be obligated in Borei Nefashot or Al HaGafen, swallow a revi'it quickly and continuously in those few seconds rather than nursing it slowly
â€¢ Hot coffee, tea, or soup: drinking that fast is difficult, so common practice is no after-blessing when you only sip slowly â€” unless you set aside at least a revi'it and drink it at once when cool enough

Drinks only â€” which after-blessing?
â€¢ Wine or grape juice: Al HaGafen (×¢Ö·×œ ×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ ×•Ö°×¢Ö·×œ ×¤Ö°Ö¼×¨Ö´×™ ×”Ö·×’Ö¸Ö¼×¤Ö¶×Ÿ) if a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
â€¢ Water, milk, soda, etc.: Borei Nefashot if a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
â€¢ Mezonot snack plus wine at the same sitting: one Al HaMichya including the wine phrase if both meet their thresholds

Where to find the exact text:
Open your siddur or bentcher to the bracha acharona section for the exact texts.

items.title: Asher Yatzar (After using the bathroom)
items.explanation:
After using the bathroom, we wash our hands and say the Asher Yatzar blessing â€” every time you finish.

When to say it:
â€¢ Normally: say Asher Yatzar each time you use the bathroom, once you are done and have washed your hands.
â€¢ If you are ill â€” for example diarrhea or another condition where you may need the bathroom again right away â€” wait until you are reasonably sure you have finished for that round before saying it. Otherwise you might say the blessing and then need the bathroom again immediately, which is impractical and not the point of the bracha.

What it is:
Asher Yatzar (×Ö²×©Ö¶××¨ ×™Ö¸×¦Ö·×¨ â€” "Who fashioned") is a blessing thanking G-d for the miraculous design of the human body. It is one of the most medically insightful prayers ever composed.

The blessing says:
G-d created the human body with wisdom â€” with openings and closings (tubes, valves, sphincters). If any one of them were to open when it should be closed, or close when it should be open, it would be impossible to survive.

Why this is profound:
Modern medicine has confirmed exactly this. A blocked ureter, a stuck heart valve, or a blocked bowel â€” any of these is a life-threatening emergency. Every time your body functions normally, it is a miracle. Asher Yatzar trains us to recognize it as one.

How to do it:
1. After using the bathroom, wash your hands (without a blessing, unless this is your morning washing)
2. Say Asher Yatzar â€” it takes about 20 seconds

Find the text in any Jewish prayer book (siddur) or bentcher.

items.title: Kisui Rosh (Hair covering for married women)
items.explanation:
Married Jewish women in Orthodox communities traditionally cover their hair in public and in the presence of men other than their husband.

What it is:
Kisui rosh (×›Ö´Ö¼×™×¡Ö¼×•Ö¼×™ ×¨Ö¹××©× â€” literally "covering of the head") is one of the fundamental practices of Jewish married life across Orthodox communities.

Halachic status:
Poskim disagree on its source. Shulchan Aruch (Even HaEzer 115:4) requires married women to cover their hair. The practice is widely accepted in Orthodox life; ask your rav for guidance.

Why:
In communities that require covering, a married woman's hair is treated as something private that should not be publicly displayed. Covering expresses that her deepest beauty is reserved for the privacy of her home and her husband. It is also an expression of tzniut (modesty) â€” the Jewish value of inner dignity over external display.

Methods of covering:
â€¢ Tichel (×˜Ö´×™×›Ö¶×œ) â€” a headscarf or wrap, commonly used across many communities
â€¢ Sheitel (×©Öµ××™×™×˜Ö¶×œ) â€” a wig; widely used in Ashkenazic and Chassidic communities
â€¢ Hat or beret
â€¢ Mitpachot â€” decorative wraps and turbans
â€¢ Combination (e.g., small wig with a hat over it)

Standards:
Most Orthodox authorities require the entire hair to be covered in public. Details vary by community. Speak with your rabbi or rebbetzin about what applies in your situation.

items.title: Tzniut (Modest dress and conduct)
items.explanation:
Tzniut is one of the defining values of Jewish life â€” it governs how we dress and how we carry ourselves in the world.

What tzniut is:
Tzniut (×¦Ö°× Ö´×™×¢×•Ö¼×ª â€” modesty) comes from the verse "Walk humbly (hatznea lechet) with your G-d" (Micah 6:8). It is not just a dress code â€” it is a philosophy of life. Tzniut means reserving what is most precious and intimate for those who truly know you, rather than displaying it to the world.

In dress (for women):
â€¢ Skirts or dresses that cover the knees
â€¢ Tops that cover the elbows and collarbone
â€¢ Avoid tight-fitting or sheer clothing
â€¢ Stockings / tights as required by community custom

In conduct (for everyone):
â€¢ Speak with dignity and thoughtfulness
â€¢ Do not seek unnecessary attention
â€¢ Act in a way that reflects inner character over outer appearance
â€¢ Lower the eyes when appropriate

For men:
Tzniut applies to men too â€” in dress, speech, and conduct. Men should not wear revealing clothing and should conduct themselves with dignity.

Standards vary by community. The key is sincere progress and consistency, guided by your local rabbi and rebbetzin.

items.title: Taharat HaMishpacha (Family Purity Laws)
items.explanation:
The laws of family purity govern the intimate relationship between husband and wife and are among the most important mitzvot in the Torah.

What they are:
Taharat HaMishpacha (×˜Ö¸×”Ö³×¨Ö·×ª ×”Ö·×žÖ´Ö¼×©Ö°××¤Ö¸Ö¼×—Ö¸×” â€” literally "family purity") is a cycle of physical separation and mikveh immersion that structures the intimacy of Jewish marriage.

The basic cycle:
â€¢ During niddah (× Ö´×“Ö¸Ö¼×” â€” menstrual period and its aftermath), the husband and wife refrain from physical contact
â€¢ After the period ends, the wife counts seven "clean days" (shivah nekiyim) during which she checks that bleeding has fully stopped
â€¢ After the seven clean days, she immerses in a mikveh (ritual pool)
â€¢ After immersion, the couple resumes their relationship

Why these laws:
Jewish tradition teaches that these laws bring holiness, freshness, and blessing to marriage. Couples who practice them often describe the monthly rhythm of separation and reunion as one of the most surprisingly positive aspects of Jewish life.

How to learn:
This system is complex and must be learned properly before marriage â€” or as soon as you begin observance. Take a Taharat HaMishpacha course from a qualified teacher (rebbetzin, kallah teacher, or through organizations like Nishmat or local Jewish community programs).

items.title: Prepare for Shabbat (Erev Shabbat checklist)
items.explanation:
Shabbat is the holiest day of the week â€” the Jewish Sabbath. It begins at sunset on Friday and ends when three stars appear on Saturday night (approximately 25 hours).

What to prepare before Shabbat:
1. Cooking: All cooking must be done before Shabbat begins. A blech (a metal sheet covering the stove flame) or a pre-set hot plate keeps food warm on Shabbat without cooking.
2. Timers: Electric lights, the hot plate, and other appliances can be left on or set to turn on/off using a Shabbos timer (pre-set before Shabbat).
3. Lay out what you need: siddur (prayer book), Chumash (Torah book), clothes, children's items â€” anything you will want.
4. Candles: Light Shabbat candles 18 minutes before sunset (see next item).

On Shabbat â€” things we do NOT do:
â€¢ Write, draw, or erase
â€¢ Cook, bake, or light fire
â€¢ Turn electricity on or off (including phones, lights, and appliances)
â€¢ Drive or ride in a motorized vehicle
â€¢ Handle money or engage in commerce

On Shabbat â€” what we DO:
â€¢ Attend synagogue for Friday night and Saturday morning services
â€¢ Say Kiddush â€” a blessing over wine sanctifying Shabbat
â€¢ Eat three festive meals (Friday night, Saturday afternoon, and a third before nightfall)
â€¢ Study Torah, sing Shabbat songs (zemiroth), rest, and spend time with family

items.title: Hadlakat Nerot (Shabbat candle lighting)
items.explanation:
Lighting candles on Friday afternoon is one of the most beloved practices in all of Judaism â€” it officially welcomes Shabbat into the home.

What it is:
Hadlakat Nerot (×”Ö·×“Ö°×œÖ¸×§Ö·×ª × Öµ×¨×•Ö¹×ª â€” lighting of candles) is a rabbinic mitzvah performed weekly before Shabbat. The Shabbat candles bring light and peace into the home, honoring the day's holiness.

Who lights:
Traditionally the woman of the household lights. If there is no woman in the home, a man lights. Ashkenazi and Chabad custom: young girls and single daughters in the home may light their own candles with a bracha (often one candle per child). Sephardic custom (Shulchan Arukh; Rav Ovadia Yosef): daughters living under their parents' roof do not recite a bracha on their own candles if the mother has already lit â€” the mother's candles establish light for the home; a duplicate bracha would be a bracha levatala.

When to light:
Exactly 18 minutes before sunset on Friday (some Ashkenazic communities light 20-22 minutes before; Sephardic communities often light closer to sunset). The app shows the candle-lighting time for your location.

How many candles:
â€¢ The minimum is two candles â€” one for Zachor ("remember" Shabbat, from Exodus 20:8) and one for Shamor ("guard" Shabbat, from Deuteronomy 5:12)
â€¢ Many women add one candle per child

The blessing:
There are two customs:
â€¢ Ashkenazi custom: Light the candles first, then wave hands over the flames, cover the eyes, recite the blessing, and uncover. Lighting is done first because reciting the blessing is considered accepting Shabbat â€” after which relighting would be forbidden. Covering the eyes allows the blessing to precede benefiting from the light.
â€¢ Sephardi custom: Recite the blessing first, then light the candles.
Follow your family's custom. When in doubt, ask your rabbi.

The blessing: "Baruch Atah Ado-nai Eloheinu Melech ha'olam, asher kid'shanu b'mitzvotav v'tzivanu l'hadlik ner shel Shabbat."

Important:
Once you have lit the candles and said the blessing, Shabbat has begun for you. Do not light additional fire or use electricity after this point.

items.title: Melave Malka (festive meal after Shabbat)
items.explanation:
Melave Malka (×žÖ°×œÖ·×•Ö¶Ö¼×” ×žÖ·×œÖ°×›Ö¸Ö¼×”) means "escorting the queen" â€” a meal Saturday night to escort the Shabbat Queen as she departs, the way one would honor a beloved guest at the door (Talmud Shabbat 119b). It is also called Seudat David HaMelech: King David held a Motzei Shabbat feast each week after learning he would die on Shabbat (Shabbat 30a).

When:
After Havdalah, from tzeit (nightfall) until dawn Sunday. Men and women; all nusachim.

What to do:
â€¢ Spread a clean tablecloth and set the table â€” the sages say to set the table even if you will eat only a small amount
â€¢ Sit and eat at the table. The ideal is a meal with bread: wash hands (netilat yadayim), say hamotzi, and eat. If you are too full for bread, you can still fulfill the mitzvah with cake or another meaningful portion of food â€” netilat yadayim applies only when you eat bread
â€¢ When you can, prepare something for this meal rather than only finishing Shabbat leftovers
â€¢ Many light candles on or near the table

Four candles (optional â€” Baal Shem Tov, Siddur Tzelota D'Yisrael; Sefer Amrei Esh):
Some light four candles at Melave Malka. Chassidic tradition teaches that King David is spiritually present at this meal. A common practice (Rabbi Aaron Roth, Shulchan HaTahor): dedicate one candle each to King David, Eliyahu HaNavi, Rabbi Meir Baal HaNess, and the Baal Shem Tov â€” or choose any four tzaddikim. Light them at the table, name each dedication, and ask then for your needs for the week ahead in the merit of the tzaddikim.

If you are truly too full from Shabbat, you are not required to force yourself â€” but even a little food at a set table honors the mitzvah.
